coredata.dc:title: Development of durable multifunctional textiles by application of carbon quantum dots synthesized from post-consumer cellulosic waste
coredata.dc:creator: Naz, Sehrish
Ali, Mumtaz
Ashraf, Munir
Riaz, Shagufta
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), are emerging eco-friendly fluorescent materials; however, their complex separation techniques and chemical involvement are limitations in their scalable production. The potential of cellulose was explored for the first time; by successfully converting post-consumer cotton waste to CQDs by facile wet pyrolysis. The process is toxic chemicals free, with a production yield of 80 % with a short synthesis time: as CQDs were synthesized via solvent-induced precipitation and filtration. Hydrothermally synthesized CQDs showed a characteristic high Stokes shift (i.e. strong green emission), and broad absorbance range. The structural and morphological characteristics as well as the particle size of synthesized CQDs were evaluated through FTIR, TEM, XPS, and XRD. The as-prepared CQDs were loaded on cotton fabric, and cationized before loading for durable functionality. Electrokinetic potential was considered for surface charge assessment after cationization. FTIR and TEM analysis were performed to confirm the successful application of CQDs on cotton fabric. UV-protection factor (UPF) and antibacterial efficacy of treated fabric against S. aureus were assessed qualitatively and quantitatively showing UPF+50 and antibacterial activity >88 %, respectively, retained even after multiple washing cycles. Therefore, the current study has proposed a facile, cost-effective, and eco-sustainable method for the development of multi-functional textiles by application of CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Cotton fabric
UV-protection factor
Antibacterial activity
Multi-functional textile

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CQDs from post-consumer waste
Testing of untreated and treated fabric
Ethics approval and consent to participate

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The funding for this research work was provided by the Higher Education Commission (HEC), Pakistan under the NRPU project [grant no. 15676].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Process of synthesis of CQDs to application on cotton fabric.
The reaction scheme represents the functionalization of quantum dots and its application on cotton fabric.
NMR (1H and C13) spectra of CQDs.
Characterization of CQDs using (a) TEM, (b) WA-XRD, and (c) FTIR.
Characterization of CQDs using a) XPS, b) XPS high-resolution scans in C1 s, and c) XPS high-resolution scans in O 1S.
XRD of untreated cotton fabric and CQDs treated cotton fabric.
The size distribution map of the CQDs
FTIR spectrum of CQDs coated cotton fabric treated and untreated

The optical properties of CQDs (a) UV-visible absorbance showing two absorbance bands, a high energy band at the wavelength range of 250 nm to 360 nm and comparatively weak absorption peak at 420 nm (b) Photoluminescence under UV light with excitation at 365 nm.
Application of CQDs as (a) visible light photocatalyst, (b,c,d) secret or fluorescent ink.
SEM images of fabrics a) before treatment with CQDs, b) After treatment with CQDs.
Qualitative antibacterial analysis of samples E1, E2, and untreated control (C) samples.
Quantitative antibacterial results for samples C (Control), T (Treated unwashed), and W (Treated washed) at 0 h and 24 h.
UPF values of treated and untreated fabrics.
UV-absorption spectra of fabrics a) treated unwashed, and b) treated washed.
Bacterial reduction percentage of control, treated unwashed, and treated washed fabric against S. aureus

head.ce:title: Development of durable multifunctional textiles by application of carbon quantum dots synthesized from post-consumer cellulosic waste

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Textile Engineering, School of Engineering and Textile, National Textile University, Sheikhupura Road, (37610), Pakistan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Textile Engineering, School of Engineering and Textile, National Textile University, Sheikhupura Road, (37610), Pakistan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Culture and Design, Clothing Technology, HTW Berlin –University of Applied Sciences for Technology and Economics Berlin, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Culture and Design, Clothing Technology
HTW Berlin –University of Applied Sciences for Technology and Economics Berlin

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Culture and Design, Clothing Technology, HTW Berlin –University of Applied Sciences for Technology and Economics Berlin, Germany.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), are emerging eco-friendly fluorescent materials; however, their complex separation techniques and chemical involvement are limitations in their scalable production. The potential of cellulose was explored for the first time; by successfully converting post-consumer cotton waste to CQDs by facile wet pyrolysis. The process is toxic chemicals free, with a production yield of 80 % with a short synthesis time: as CQDs were synthesized via solvent-induced precipitation and filtration. Hydrothermally synthesized CQDs showed a characteristic high Stokes shift (i.e. strong green emission), and broad absorbance range. The structural and morphological characteristics as well as the particle size of synthesized CQDs were evaluated through FTIR, TEM, XPS, and XRD. The as-prepared CQDs were loaded on cotton fabric, and cationized before loading for durable functionality. Electrokinetic potential was considered for surface charge assessment after cationization. FTIR and TEM analysis were performed to confirm the successful application of CQDs on cotton fabric. UV-protection factor (UPF) and antibacterial efficacy of treated fabric against S. aureus were assessed qualitatively and quantitatively showing UPF+50 and antibacterial activity >88 %, respectively, retained even after multiple washing cycles. Therefore, the current study has proposed a facile, cost-effective, and eco-sustainable method for the development of multi-functional textiles by application of CQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The textile and apparel industries are among the most significant consumer goods industries, whereas it is also considered one of the most environmentally polluting industries. Post-industrial or post-consumer textile wastes have high climatic, social, and environmental hazards. U.S. Environmental Protection Agency presented a report in which it was stated that different pressures like resource inputs, land & chemicals, textile waste, and discharge of greenhouse gases impact the whole textile system in every phase from the production of textile fibers, development and distribution of textile products, use of these products, recycling and reuse until the final waste management [1].

In advanced countries with innovative technology, the recycling of clothes is so limited, and in countries like Pakistan waste management or recycling is not effective. Textile recycling routes are usually chemical, mechanical, and thermal. Cotton covers about 76 % of study material and is the highest studied material for reuse and recycling, but, most of the recycled materials are polymer, fiber, or fabric and not the monomer [2,3]. Textile waste recycling in the form of fibers, polymers, or even monomers is still far from commercial viability. Most of the textile waste has been used for downcycling but the upcycling of textile waste has never been studied in which textile waste is hydrolyzed to synthesize nanomaterials i.e., graphene/carbon quantum dots in an economical way, and its application on cotton fabric to make it multifunctional. Consumers nowadays rather than aesthetic purpose or body covering are also interested in the performance and multifunctionality of a textile. That is why the demand for multifunctional and smart textiles is inflating day by day [4]. Textile waste can be converted to advanced materials and products by upcycling, one of the examples is quantum dots that are produced from the post-consumer textile waste. Recently, graphene/carbon quantum dots are considered as the smallest nano-scaled graphene particles, generally smaller than 10 nm in size [5]. These zero-dimensional structures with good biocompatibility, high photostability, a large number of easily substitutable functional groups, and low toxicity have been grabbing the attention of researchers in fields like solar cells, sensors, bioimaging, and textile as well [6,7]. Graphene or carbon quantum dots have good antibacterial and antiviral properties [8]. For CQD's synthesis several methods are used one of them which is quite popular nowadays is a hydrothermal method, the main advantage of this process is it can be executed easily. The hydrothermal process is carried out in an autoclave, where direct heat is provided to aqueous solutions with correct precursors at high temperatures [9].

Photocatalysis is an influential property of quantum dots to degrade organic pollutants under UV-light and visible-light irradiation [10,11]. In this pitch, green emissive graphene/carbon quantum dots synthesis is a new area of research with the least environmental impression. By green synthesis, the quantum dots have been synthesized and used to degrade organic dyes subjected to visible light [12,13]. However, very few research articles are available on the synthesis of quantum dots using natural material as a precursor. Curcumin-based carbon quantum dots showed good antiviral properties under photodynamic effects [14,15]. Fungi-based quantum dots can be used for various applications like bio-sensing, targeted drug delivery photocatalysis, etc. [16]. In another study, fluorescent NS-CQDs were derived from bananas through the single-step hydrothermal process for the identification of copper ions [17].

In another study, the green synthesis approach is so prominent due to an environmentally friendly method and does not contain toxic chemicals at the experimental stage. This study used Sideritis vuralii (S. vuralii), an endemic in Turkey, as a source of the green synthesis approach. The carbon quantum dot (CQDs) particles were synthesized with the hydrothermal method from the S. vuralii plant [18]. All these methods are feasible for lab-scale production; however, industrial-scale production needs a facile process with high production yields. In short, CQDs found different applications in various fields. Some studies on antimicrobials include a combination of arylidene hydrazinyl moiety with pyrido[2,3-d]pyrimidin-4-one skeleton in compounds results in the output of unprecedented anti-microbial agents [19]. A comparative study was proposed to show the effect of variation in the heteroatoms in the main skeleton of CQDs proveniences, on their affinity for nucleation of CQDs, as anti-inflammatory and anticancer drugs [20]. In another study, one of the important chemical agents in designing anti-convulsant, antiatherosclerotic, and anti-cancer drugs is pyridine derivatives. Alkyl and aryl derivatives of cyanopyridones show antimicrobial, antihypertensive, anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and antipyretic properties as well as 1KK-b inhibitor properties [21]. Additionally, in other studies, titanium-based MOFs (MIL-125-NH2) were enhanced with silver molybdate (Ag2MoO4) and silver vanadate (AgVO3) nanocrystals. The component of joining Ag2MoO4 and AgVO3 into the MIL-125-NH2 network has been examined. The expansion of our work focuses on examining the effect of MOFs on both antimicrobial and anticancer agents [22]. While studying photocatalytic activity, in a study Phthalocyanines (PCs) are promising materials in photocatalytic reactions because of their unique adsorption and photosensitizing properties. Three phthalocyanines (Pcs) derivatives with Al3+ and Co2+ ions as the central metal were prepared to attach them to the MOF matrix. The photocatalytic decay performance of methylene blue (MB) and methyl orange (MO) and their mixtures were investigated under visible light illumination. The photocatalyst NH2-MIL-125, Al-PcCl@NH2-MIL-125, CoPc@NH2-MIL-125, and CoPcPA@NH2-MIL-125 show 0.01, 0.012, 0.035, and 0.046 min−1 MB degradation rate, respectively [23].

In the current study, a single-step simple technique has been created for the scalable production of CQDs from post-consumer cotton waste by wet pyrolysis using environmentally friendly solvents. The major bottleneck of the facile synthesis, without the use of any additional chemicals, and high production yield, make the proposed strategy highly attractive for the industrial scale production of CQDs for several practices, but, specifically, for the applications with high consumption, such as textile functionalization in a continuous process. Synthesized CQDs when coated onto cationized cotton fabric by pad cure method exhibited durable functionalities such as antibacterial activity, photocatalysis of dye, and remarkable shielding against the hazardous effect of UV radiation.

Post-consumer waste was procured from local industry in Faisalabad. Citric acid ( reagent, ≥99.5 %), Acetic acid (≥99 %), Cationizing agent/coupling agent (Mercaptopropyltris(methyloxy)silane (95 %) purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Methylene Blue N (Dye content ∼70 %, Sigma Aldrich). All chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Nutrient agar and broth were acquired from Sigma Aldrich (analytical grade). Bactria was provided by Agriculture University Faisalabad.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs from post-consumer waste
ce:section.ce:para: The whole procedure of synthesis and application on cotton fabric is presented in Scheme 1 . In this method, one-step hydrolysis of cotton was performed, and through this process, cotton fabric was directly converted into CQDs. For cotton hydrolysis, a mixture containing acetic acid, citric acid, and water was prepared in a ratio of 25:25:50, respectively. While 2 g post-consumer cotton fabric was shredded into small pieces and added to this solution. This reaction mixture was subjected to a hydrothermal process in a preheated oven at 220 °C for 6 h. The resultant filtered solution of CQDs was visualized under UV light to check the glow of the solution. The glowing solution is indicative of the successful formation of CQDs. The quantum dots synthesized in this method were dark brown color shown in Fig. 1a and the glowing solution of CQDs is shown in Fig. 1b. The CQDs dispersion was dialyzed for one week with a dialysis membrane having a molecular weight of 14 kDa for purity and removal of other non-required materials. The resultant solution was then subjected to a rotary evaporator for the removal of solvent from the reaction mass.

A thick mass of CQDs as shown in Fig. 2 was obtained after rotary evaporation of solution. The resultant thick mass was then saved for further analysis. The hydrothermal method was enhanced by utilizing elevated temperatures and pressures, which facilitate the breakdown of cellulose. Incorporating acidic catalysts significantly aids in hydrolyzing cellulose chains, making them more responsive to conversion into CQDs. Additionally, controlling the reaction time, and extended durations allows for more complete decomposition of cellulose. By integrating these approaches—high-temperature hydrothermal treatment, the use of acid catalysts, optimal reaction times, and effective pre-treatments—the stability of cellulose and achieve high yields of CQDs is achievable. In various synthesis methods, such as hydrothermal technique, citric acid facilitates the formation of CQDs by undergoing dehydration and polymerization, leading to the creation of carbon-rich structures. The presence of functional groups in citric acid, such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, enhances the stability and solubility of the CQDs in aqueous solutions, which is essential for their application in fluorescence and sensing technologies. Furthermore, the optimization of synthesis parameters, including temperature and reaction time, can significantly improve the quantum yield of CQDs.

The synthesized CQDs were then applied to cotton fabric. For this purpose, the cotton fabric was pre-treated with a cationizing agent for the generation of reactive groups on the surface of the cotton fabric.

For fabric pre-treatment, 2 g of cationizing agent (mercaptopropyltris(methyloxy)silane) was added to 100 mL of distilled water. Then, 2 g of cotton fabric was dipped into this solution. The solution containing cotton fabric was then placed in an orbital shaker for 6 h at 50 °C. After 6 h, the beaker was removed from the orbital shaker. The fabric was then removed out of the solution and rinsed with water followed by drying for 1 min in the oven at 100 °C. The dried fabric was ready for the application of CQDs.

The CQDs were applied on cotton fabric in a dual step. In the initial step, the pre-treated cotton fabric was dipped in the 4 % solution of CQDs. The solution containing fabric was then placed in an orbital shaker for 6 h at 50 °C. The fabric was then removed from the solution and rinsed with water followed by drying for 1 min in the oven at 100 °C (half of the sample was saved as control and labeled as C). After that, the remaining solution of CQDs was coated on fabric via a pad-dry-cure procedure. The fabric was dipped in the solution 5–10 times and dried at 100 °C after each dip followed by curing at 170 °C for 3 min after the last dip. The dried and cured fabric (E1) was brown. The synthesis process of CQDS from post-consumer cotton waste and its application on cotton fabric is presented in Fig. 1. In the next studies, the other textile waste will be used to synthesize CQDs for textile functionality. During hydrothermal synthesis, the formation of multiple aromatic ring structures in carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is linked to the pyrolysis of organic precursors, which leads to the formation of a conjugated system of carbon atoms. During this process, high temperature is responsible for the breakdown of the organic molecules, enabling the fusion of smaller aromatic compounds into larger graphene-like structures where sp2 hybridized crystals in sp3 hybridized carbon skeleton are formed [24]. CQDs exhibit a unique structural composition characterized by their amorphous nature, which significantly influences their properties. The core structure of CQDs primarily consists of sp³-hybridized carbon, creating a disordered arrangement that lacks long-range order. This amorphous framework is interspersed with sp²-hybridized atomic domains, which emerge during the synthesis process. The amorphous core of CQDs is predominantly made up of sp³-hybridized carbon atoms. This structure provides stability and contributes to the overall rigidity of the CQD. Within this core, sp²-hybridized domains are present, forming distinct shapes that contribute to the optical characteristics of the dots. These sp² domains are likely to appear on the surface due to their lower formation energy, enhancing their accessibility to light. Unlike typical flat aromatic dimers, the sp²-hybridized domains within CQDs are nonplanar. This nonplanarity is crucial as it affects how these domains interact with light. The closely spaced layers of these nonplanar domains are embedded within the rigid amorphous core, creating a complex structure that facilitates unique optical responses [25].

A detailed analysis of CQDs using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL JEM 2100F) in transmission mode was carried out to determine the size distribution and morphology. Wide-angle X-ray diffraction (WA-XRD) was carried out to inspect the CQDs solution, cotton untreated fabric, and cotton fabric treated with CQDs using a Rigaku Denki X-ray generator (Rigaku D/MAX-2500) with Cu Kα radiation. Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy investigated the functional groups of surfaces. FTIR spectrum was recorded with an FTIR spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) in the 400–4000 cm−1 spectral range at room temperature. CQDs' chemical compositions were evaluated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) from Thermo ScientificK-Alpha. The spectra of samples were recorded by an aluminum anode (Al Kα= 1486.6 eV). The UV protection factor (UPF) of CQDs coated fabric was examined using an HD902C (Nantong Hongda) anti-ultraviolet penetration test system. Each sample was examined in 5 distinct front and back locations before the average value was determined. The UV protection factor for both the treated and untreated fabrics (unwashed and washed) was determined according to AATCC- 183 standard testing methods

ce:section.ce:section-title: Testing of untreated and treated fabric

ce:section.ce:para: To measure the surface charge of cationized cotton fabric using a technique that includes current or streaming potential, the Anton-Paar SurPASS Electrokinetic Analyzer was used. Because of the interaction of forces between the textile surface and the solution applied to the textiles, the surface charge affects the absorption and absorption of surfactants, finishes, and dyes. An electrolyte solution (0.01 M KCl) was run over fabric samples to detect the surface charge, providing a value known as zeta potential. The surface charge was then computed using a back-titration approach. The ions in the electrolyte solution interact with the charged sample surface throughout the flow process. As a result, a layer of ions generated was drawn to the sample surface, and a subsequent layer of ions was repelled by a surface bearing the same charge. Electrodes mounted close to the sample cell monitored the charge difference, which was used to calculate the zeta potential together with the flow process and other well-known electrolyte solution parameters that determine whether the surface charge is negative or positive.

Treated cotton fabrics with a sample diameter of 3 cm underwent dye degradation by putting the sample in a beaker containing 20 mL of ethylene Blue N dye solution (2 mg/L). The beaker was placed in visible light (12 W, Phillips x 4) and a UV light box (at maximum emission of 368 nm). After every 30 mins, the absorbance was determined to assess the degradation of dye by using a UV–Vis NIR spectrometer (2D Detector Module, PerkinElmer, Lambda 950) at λmax (665 nm).

UV–vis spectroscopy assessments were directed by using Perkin-Elmer (Lambda EZ-210), wavelength from 8000 to 200 nm, using a quartz cuvette in transmission mode. The CQDs reaction was observed using the absorption spectra creation and relative colloidal stability in the medium. Furthermore, based on the curve's “absorbance onset” the optical properties can also be calculated. All the trials were run in triplet unless specific results were noted.

The testing method AATCC-147 performed a qualitative assessment of samples, generally mentioned as the disc diffusion method. Fabric samples were cut in the form of a circular disc and an autoclave sterilized at 121 °C. After that samples were put down on a 25 mL agar plate that were inoculated with a bacterial culture of one of both strains such as S. aureus and E. coli. The sample stacked agar plates were then incubated at 37 °C for 24 hrs. Afterward, the inhibition zones were calculated according to the following formula:
(1)
Zone
of
inhibition
( mm )
=
D 2 − D 1
2
Where D1 is used for the diameter of the sample disc while D2 is the diameter of the circular zone including the sample disc, where no bacterial growth was seen.

In this method, agar plates were inoculated with 1 mL of bacterial culture (2 × 108 CFU/ml), and respective samples (control, unwashed, and washed treated fabrics) were positioned on these inoculated plates. The samples were applied 200 g cylindrical weight for 1 min. After 1 min, samples were detached from the agar plate surface, placed on an empty petri dish with an inverted position, and incubated at 37 °C for 18 h. After 24 h, the incubated fabric samples were taken in 20 mL saline solution and vortex for 1 min which was then serially diluted to 104. Agar plates were prepared and 60 µL of every dilution was spread on the agar surface and all plates were placed for incubation. The same procedure was followed at zero h. To trial at 0 h, the incubation was not performed after bacteria contacted the fabric. The testing was done in replicates and standard deviations (SD%) were estimated. The number of bacterial colonies in logarithmic value of colony-forming unit per mL (CFU/mL) after inoculating at 0 h and 18 h was counted to determine the log-reduction (in terms of activity A) and percentage reduction using equations given below,
(2)
%
R e d u c t i o n =
[
(
l o g
C t −
l o g
T t
)
/
l o g
C t
]
* 100
(3)
A = F − G

Where F= (log Ct - logC0) and G= (log Tt – log T0) and C0 and T0 represent the bacterial count of control and treated fabric at 0 h respectively and Ct and Tt are the bacterial count of control and treated samples after 24 h respectively while A is the antibacterial activity value.

Nanomaterials do not show any affinity towards textiles, to increase their binding with textiles they must undergo a pretreatment process. In the case of synthesized carbon quantum dots, cotton fabric was functionalized. Then they were applied to the fabric by a conventional pad-dry-cure method. Surface salinization is a process in which different silanes have been used to modify the quantum dots’ surface in this study mercaptopropyltris(methyloxy)silane was used to increase the binding of CQDs with the textiles [26]Thus, the textile's durable functionality was attained. Scheme 1 gives the whole reaction scheme of functionalization and application on cotton fabric.

NMR and Raman spectroscopy were employed to verify the chemical structure and molecular composition, with particular attention to the degree of crystallinity and the sp²/sp³ carbon ratio. A high sp²/sp³ ratio suggests a predominantly graphitic or crystalline nature of the material. This indicates that the carbon atoms are well-organized, forming an extensive π-conjugated system that enhances electron delocalization and structural stability. Consequently, such materials exhibit a high crystallinity level, characterized by prominent graphitic Raman peaks like the G-band near 1580 cm−1, as illustrated in Figs. 2 and 3 . The sp²-hybridized carbon atoms, which form double bonds, are integral to graphitic or graphene-like structures. These atoms are arranged in a planar hexagonal lattice, where each is bonded to three others, as depicted in Scheme 1.

The NMR spectra reveal the presence of four distinct chemical environments across different regions, as outlined below. In the 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. 2), the identified regions are as follows: 1–3 ppm (for sp³ C–H protons), 3–6 ppm (for protons linked to hydroxyl, ether, and carbonyl groups), 6–8 ppm (for aromatic or sp² protons), and 8–10 ppm (for aldehydic protons). Similarly, in the 13C NMR spectrum (Fig. 2), four corresponding regions are noted: 20–80 ppm (for sp³ carbons and those associated with hydroxyl groups), 80–100 ppm (for carbons with ether linkages), 100–120 ppm (for sp² or aromatic carbons), and 175–190 ppm (for carbonyl carbons) [27].

Fig. 3 presents the Raman spectra of the CQDs. Through Raman spectroscopy, characteristic bands of CQDs were identified following the deconvolution of the spectrum. The bands observed at approximately 1252 cm−1 and 1481 cm−1 are referred to as the D and G bands, respectively. These bands indicate the presence of poly-aromatic hydrocarbons embedded in the carbon skeleton. The D band is associated with carbon atoms exhibiting sp³ hybridization, while the G band corresponds to those with sp² hybridization. Notably, valuable insights can be derived from the ratio of the intensities of the D and G bands (ID/IG ratio). For the CQDs sample analyzed, this ratio was determined to be 0.78, indicating a predominance of carbon atoms with sp² hybridization, which is typical for carbon structures found in CQDs. Furthermore, a ratio below unity suggests a greater presence of well-ordered carbon domains compared to defective ones [28].

The post-consumer cellulosic waste was directly converted to CQDs by hydrothermal method. The size was confirmed by the transmission mode TEM analysis, which shows that CQDs are crystalline spherical dots, with size varying between 2 – 7 nm with an average size between 4 and 5 nm as shown in Fig. 4a ) [29]. The crystal structure of CQDs was studied with WA-XRD, which showed a broad peak at 20.5° (Fig. 4b), which indicates the interlayer spacing (d) of 0.43 nm. The broader peak in 2ϴ corresponding to the (002) hkl plane confirms the formation of a very small carbogenic core which concludes that the broad peak hinted at the formation of very tiny CQDs. According to the Scherrer equation, the size of crystallite tends to decrease when XRD peaks are broadened, confirming the amorphous nature of CQDs [30,31].

These groups were analyzed with FTIR in the range of 3500–500 cm−1 as shown in Fig. 4c; a broad peak between 3500 and 3000 cm−1 of the OH group is present in the spectrum, another intense absorption peak at 1600 cm−1 of C = O of citric acid and HOC = O peak present between the region 1320–1210 cm−1, other peaks between 1250 and 1100 cm−1 of CO-C, and 1430–1300 cm−1 of H-C = O type functional groups confirm the presence of carboxylic acid group [32,33].

These functional groups were further analyzed with XPS in Fig. 5a , b& c, which showed two peaks in a single image for C 1 s, and O 1s. Further, high-resolution XPS scans for C 1 s (Fig. 5b) and O 1 s (Fig. 5c), at binding energies of 285 eV and 530 eV, respectively. Peak at 285 eV can be fitted in comparison with CQDs, which is recognized to the C = O group. The fitted peak at 530 from the O 1 s spectrum is ascribed to the C–OH/C–O–C group. Typically, the atomic ratio was analyzed to be 29 % carbon and 71 % oxygen, showing that CQDs are highly enriched with oxygenated functional groups. From these characterizations, it can be proved that CQDs are suitable in size, and circular shape; and are highly enriched with oxygenated functional groups [34].

The crystalline nature of cellulose and CQDs was studied by XRD as presented in Fig. 6 . The X-ray pattern of the untreated cotton fabric exhibits the sharp peak of the crystalline structure at 2ɵ angle at 14.8°, 16.3°, and 22.5° however the hkl indices are present at (002), (110), and (200). Also, the peak of fabric treated with CQDs is at similar 2ɵ angles at 14.8°, 16.3° and 21.1°. The peak appeared at 16.3° and showed high intensity in the treated sample. Hence the CQDs were coated by cationizing the cotton fabric and the binder were also used for attaching CQDs on the surface. Therefore, the difference in minor peak indicated that CQDs did not damage the crystal structure of cellulose, and as the cellulose content is present in bulk compared to it the CQDs content present is almost negligible [31]. Compared to the crystal structure and size of cotton fiber, the size of CQDs is negligible, therefore prominent difference in XRD was not observed.

Fig. 5 shows the spectrum of cotton fiber and typical bands of cellulose, lignin, and hemicellulose are observed. The strong peak observed at 3350 cm−1 is characteristic of the hydroxyl (OH) groups of cellulose, lignin, and water. The peak at 2896 cm−1 is characteristic of the stretching vibration of CH present in cellulose and hemicellulose, and the band at 1622 cm−1 may be related to the presence of water in the fibers [35].

The differences in the descriptions of the crystal structure of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) obtained from transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyses stem from the distinct aspects of CQD structure that each technique emphasizes. TEM offers high-resolution images of individual CQDs, enabling direct visualization of their morphology. When TEM characterizes CQDs as "crystalline spherical dots," it typically refers to observable features and local ordering within the CQD structure. This technique can identify small, well-defined domains or regions that exhibit some degree of crystallinity, even if the overall structure is not entirely crystalline. Conversely, XRD provides insights into the average crystal structure by measuring the scattering of X-rays from the atomic planes within the sample. A typical XRD pattern for crystalline materials displays distinct peaks that correspond to specific planes in the crystal lattice. However, if CQDs are primarily amorphous or show only short-range order without long-range periodicity, the XRD pattern will lack well-defined peaks, instead presenting a broad, diffuse halo. This halo indicates an amorphous or partially ordered structure, supporting the overall amorphous nature of CQDs.
The small particle size of CQDs as shown by Fig. 7 can be seen that the as-synthesized CQDs are uniform in size and possess a nearly spherical shape.

The Gaussian fitting curve reveals that the average size of the CQDs is about between 4 nm and 5 nm, with an average particle size of 4.75 nm as determined by statistical analysis of more than five hundred particles by using the Image J software [36].

Fig. 8 also displays the FTIR spectrum of CQDs. The peak centered at 3440 cm−1 suggests the presence of many hydroxyl groups on the CQDs’ surface. The peak between 2900 cm−1 to 2850 cm−1 corresponds to the C–H stretch of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons. The peaks at about 1387 cm −1indicate the presence of the C − O group. These results indicate the CQDs possess a mass of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on their surfaces, which are bound by cotton fabric decreasing the intensity of peak at 3350 cm−1 and a small shift of peak at 3450 cm−1 confirming the bonding of CQDs with the cotton fabric. The FTIR spectra of the CQD-modified cotton fabric exhibited the characteristic peaks of cotton, including the hydroxyl group (3440 cm−1) and the CO-C linkage (1032 cm−1). This indicates that the incorporation of CQDs into the cotton fabric did not disrupt the inherent chemical structure of the cotton as the crystal structure of CQDs is negligible as compared to the crystal structure of cotton [37].

The optical properties of CQDs were studied by UV-visible absorbance and by fluorescence test, which showed that the CQDs have visible light absorbance and associated visible light excitable fluorescence activity. UV visible absorbance showed two absorbance bands, as highlighted in Fig. 9a . High energy band at the wavelength range of 250 nm to 360 nm refers to π-π* and n-π* transitions of core conjugated C = C/CC. However, a second band extending from near UV light to visible light, with a peak absorption at 420 nm. This visible light absorption peak is attributed to the low binding energy functional groups enriched surface of the CQDs. These functional groups present new surface states between the HOMO and LUMO bands of CQDs, thus decreasing the band edge transition energy of electrons. This transition renders a clear red shift in the absorbance band, which is evidence that wet pyrolysis makes the surface highly functional. On the other hand, cellulose-derived CQDs by dry phase pyrolysis (combustion) method show the UV–Vis absorption only in the wavelength range 250 nm - 290 nm [38,39]. Other than the red-shift of surface states dependent absorbance peak, the core absorbance peak also showed a red-shift, which is attributed to the highly crystalline and compact packing of chains in nano-cellulose. A higher conjugation degree is imparted during the pyrolysis of well-aligned chains, thus causing a red shift in the π-π* and n-π* transitions. Analyzing the excitation range of CQDs, it can be observed that the low-intensity peaks were also observed in the infrared spectrum. The emission for these low-energy excitation peaks was recorded to be anti-stokes shift type, as shown in Fig. 9b.

This upconversion ability of CQDs is observed because of simultaneous absorption of a couple or more low-energy photons, for excitation. However, the emissive recombination is a single phase, which makes the low-energy photons to be usefully utilized. The excitation of these low-energy photons is because of an extremely small gap between the energy states of the CQDs. This effect enables the utilization of a broad spectrum of light for photocatalytic and biological (bioimaging) applications [40]. The potential of, thus, developed carbon quantum was explored for the visible light-guided photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue dye, secret ink, and pH-sensitive probe. For this purpose, CQDs-coated cotton fabric was used. There is a close link between durable multifunctional textiles and photocatalytic dye degradation. The durable multifunctional textiles are going to maintain their catalytic action longer providing sustained functionality. Conversely, if the textile's durability is compromised, the photocatalytic performance may diminish, affecting the textile's overall effectiveness in dye degradation. Such textiles with antibacterial efficiency, UV protection, and dye degradation could be used potentially in medical textiles, sportswear, and different outdoor tasks.

The emission of deep UV excitation (300 nm) is centered at 450 nm, which extends to 500 nm on excitation with 365 nm. The emission centered at 450 nm is because of core excitation, and it is consistent with the calculated emission of a hybridized carbon core embedded in oxygen and carbon skeleton [41]. However, the emission upon excitation with a near UV-visible range is centered at 490 nm – 520 nm, which extends to 625 nm as the excitation wavelength is extended to 500 nm. These emissions in the near UV-visible light excitation are regarded to be because of the edge states, primarily formed as a result of high concentrations of C = O, and HOC = O [42]. The excitation dependency of such surface states generated emissions is commonly observed because of the existence of abundant intermediate energy states between HOMO-LUMO [43]. The probability of emissive recombination highly varies between these closely packed energy states, upon excitation with different energies; thus causing a red-shift and low-intensity emission upon excitation with lower energies [44]. It can be observed that CQDs show an excitation wavelength-dependent emission.

Additionally, CQDs ink can also serve as secret or fluorescent ink, for which the CQDs were applied to the cotton fabric. By applying a highly diluted solution of CQDs, there was no pattern observed in visible light (Fig. 10b), however, under UV light, different designs/textures can be observed. The CQDs absorb UV light and emit visible light, making the patterns visible only under UV light, as shown in Fig. 10 (c,d).

The surface morphology of the synthesized CQDs treated fabric was characterized by Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM). Fig. 11a and Fig. 11b show the images of CQDs treated fabric at different magnifications. The typical size of synthesized CQDs varies from 5 to 10 nm. It demonstrates cotton fabric coated with CQDs as the morphology of the cotton fabric modified with CQDs was rougher than the untreated cotton fabric. SEM images clearly showed that CQDs form a layer on the surface of cotton fiber, and bounded well with the surface of cotton fabric [47].

The ζ potential for cationized cotton fabric achieved was +1.0 mV which was −10.0 eV for pristine cotton fabric. The certain value of ζ potential is due to the presence of hydroxyl groups at the surface of cotton fabric in the wet state that changed from −10.0 mV to −2.0 mV for cationized cotton fabric due to positively charged silane i.e., mercaptopropyltris(methyloxy)silane bonded at the surface [48]. The surface charge was tested to confirm the cationization of fabric that was responsible for the electrostatic force of attraction between CQDs and cationic fabric.

The antibacterial analysis of both sample E1 (CQDs coated unwashed) and sample E2 (CQDs coated washed) was assessed through both qualitative and quantitative antibacterial analysis. For qualitative antibacterial analysis, AATCC-147, the disc diffusion method was followed, and the obtained results are shown in Fig. 12 . From the images, it was seen that there was no zone of inhibition around treated fabric samples because of the non-leaching behavior of CQDs and their binding with the cotton fabric, however, no bacterial growth was observed above, below, and around both treated fabric samples which showed that both E1 and E2 fabric samples have antibacterial activity. In the case of untreated control (C) fabric samples, there was a huge number of bacterial colonies below and above the fabric sample. For gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus) the antibacterial activity is always higher, and the CQDs have a good impact on antimicrobial activity, so no bacteria grow above and below the treated samples. The prepared CQDs have a carboxyl and hydroxyl‑rich surface which can prevent their binding to bacteria with a negative charge on the surface and cause antibacterial activity to plunge in this way CQDs significantly decline the bacteria cell destruction [29,49]. Further, antibacterial testing in which bacterial colonies were reduced as a %age was calculated to ensure the antibacterial efficacy of the treated cotton textiles.

In the current study, the Minimum Inhibitory Concentrations (MIC) were assessed by the broth micro-dilution method and the results are as shown in Fig. 13 . Both treated fabric samples washed and unwashed as well as untreated control fabric samples were tested. From the results, it was observed that the sample treated at 0 h (T0) showed>70 % reduction in bacterial growth while the sample treated at 24 h (Tt) showed>90 % reduction in bacterial growth against S. aureus.

For further clarification, images for fabric samples at 0 h and after 24 h are presented. It can be seen that the number of surviving bacterial colonies was significantly higher than inoculated bacterial colonies in the case of untreated cotton fabric which showed that there was no activity in the control fabric sample. In the case of sample T0 a significant number of bacterial colonies survived but in sample Tt the number of survived bacterial colonies is remarkably reduced from the inoculated bacterial colonies suggesting that both samples have high antibacterial action. Results demonstrate that the Carbon quantum dots covering the surface of the antibacterial fabric showed an inhibitory effect. The survival rate of S. aureus was near 0 with the treatment of Carbon Dots (CDs), signifying that the Carbon Dots had inhibitory effects on bacteria. For S. aureus CDs showed an obvious bacteriostatic effect, and approximately suppressed the bacterial growth. These results made vibrant the broad-spectrum antibacterial activity of CQDs [30,50]

Table 1 consists of the count of colonies at 0 hr. and after 24 hrs., bacterial reduction for all tested samples against S. aureus before and after washing. After 24 hrs., in treated and washed samples the bacterial reduction is observed clearly [29,49]. The activity of treated fabric could be due to the formation of ROS (Reactive Oxygen Species) that could damage through oxidative stress, protein, cell membrane, and DNA of the bacterial cells [50].

The UV protection factor for both the treated and untreated fabrics was determined according to AATCC- 183 standard testing methods. For this purpose, the fabric samples were scanned at five different locations and the readings were calculated. The acquired results are presented in Fig. 14 and Fig. 15 . From the values, it can be seen that the UPF value for the untreated cotton fabric was 7 whereas the UPF of cotton fabric treated with CQDs was 150 which showed that the fabric has blocked >98 % or UV-A and UV-B radiations. However, after 20 washing cycles the UPF value for the treated fabric reduced to 97.

After washing, this decline in UPF value could be the elimination of unfixed CQD molecules present on the surface of the fabric. Despite the significant reduction in UPF value after washing, the washed fabric still blocked >97 % of UV-radiations which revealed that a significant concentration of CQDs is present on cotton fabric even after severe repeated laundry cycles. From the results, it was concluded that excellent UV protection has been achieved through the loading of CQDs on cotton fabric. For more clarification of the above findings, the UV-transmittance spectra of washed and unwashed fabric samples are provided in Fig. 15. From the spectra, it was observed that both samples have allowed less than 2 % of UV-radiations to pass through it while blocking greater than 98 % of UV-A and UV-B rays. This strong UV absorption shown sby the cotton fabrics treated with CQDs could be due to the presence of organic moieties such as -OH, -COOH, and C = C. These groups are reported to have the ability to absorb high-energy UV-radiation [51,52].

The results from UV absorption studies further corroborate the observed antibacterial activity by highlighting a direct relationship between this efficacy and the optical properties of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Specifically, the UV absorption spectra reveal that CQDs exhibit significant absorption and excitation characteristics. These optical properties facilitate the generation of Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS), which play a crucial role in bacterial inactivation. The ROS produced through this process are highly reactive and contribute to the destruction or deactivation of bacterial cells. Thus, the UV absorption data not only confirms the presence of CQDs but also underscores their mechanism of action in antimicrobial activity through ROS-mediated bacterial deactivation.

A facile process has been established for the scalable synthesis of CQDs. The eco-friendly synthesis process was designed to achieve highly functional and visible light active green emissive CQDs, with a production yield of 95 %. The proposed CQDs possess a broad absorbance spectrum and associated excitation-dependent fluorescence. Owing to these interesting optical properties, the CQDs were tested for visible-light-driven photocatalytic degradation (80 %), green secret/fluorescent inks, and pH-sensitive probes. The CQDs were successfully synthesized from post-consumer waste followed by applied on cotton fabric that was already cationized with mercaptopropyltris(methyloxy)silane with improved binding. The treated cotton fabric showed good antibacterial and UV protection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
ce:section.ce:para: N/A.
All the authors whose names have been added to the manuscript are approved for publication and they are all accountable for this research work in all aspects.
This article contained all the data, generated, or evaluated during the current study. All findings have been added in the manuscript as graphical representation and in tabular form.
The funding for this research work was provided by the Higher Education Commission (HEC), Pakistan under the NRPU project [grant no. 15676].

Sehrish Naz: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Mumtaz Ali: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Munir Ashraf: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Shagufta Riaz: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: All the authors hereby declare that they have no known personal, financial, or other interests, direct or indirect, in any matter that raises or may raise a conflict and that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this manuscript in any way.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was conducted at National Textile University, NTRC lab.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2025.141951.
